speaker: interviewer
text: --Levi. We're in London, England. And this is tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- so we stood there for first to be counted. And we stood for hours. And first we were freezing. And then it would warm up. And then, then we-- then they brought the coffee, the so-called coffee, which was some brownish lukewarm liquid, but which we didn't get in cups or mugs, individual ones. But we got it-- the first one in the row of five got it in a medium sized casserole. And now it's a fact that those who are more aggressive, they're in the first row and in the second row. And those who are more aggressive and more selfish are the ones-- and who had the most sense of survival-- they're the ones who didn't really care about, that much about others, so they drank. And then the second one drank. And the third one drank. And by the time it came to the fourth and fifth, there wasn't very much left. And I am afraid I was mostly in the fourth or fifth row. So I had very little to drink. Then we-- after that we dispersed. But by that time we have been standing for about three or four hours. And anybody who collapsed during this time was carried away and was not seen any more. And then we, we stood around or sat around in the dirt, and as I said, yellowish, grayish, sandy, not quite sand, dusty dust, which stuck onto us because we were wet, and we were clammy, and we were dirty. And we are, we're getting dirtier and dirtier. At one point, we were actually taken to have a bath. I say a bath or a shower. I think it was a shower. I think it was a shower. And we were walked to Auschwitz. Now we were walked over a road which I remember there were very, very spiky stones. The stones weren't round but they were very spiky. And anybody who stepped out-- and at one point I suddenly slipped and step out of the row. And next minute I was on the ground because someone gave me such a-- I mean hit me so strongly on my-- what's it called this was part of the-- I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back of your neck

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the neck. On my neck, yes. Sorry in French and in other languages there is a word for it. And I can't think of the English word. And on my neck that you-- that I completely-- I mean, I was giddy and I completely lost my balance. And then they kicked me. And then I managed to get up and walk on again. But-- and very careful not to step out again. But that's what happened when we were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we arrived in Auschwitz. And we were taken into a shower. And of course, whenever you were taken into a shower, you were terrified because you didn't know is it going to be water or is it going-- what's going to come out? By that time we knew that gas comes out from showers. But it was water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were given clean clothing. Again, only one piece of clothing, which wither fitted or didn't fit. And some there-- I had a very heavy black dress-- perhaps we didn't get-- I don't think we got clean clothing then. No, because I had, during my whole Auschwitz time, I had this very thick black dress, which in the heat of the day was unbearable. And it was prickly. It was of a prickly material too so that it was pretty unbearable. But it wasn't enough at night when it was freezing, and in the evening, and late afternoon, and the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, we had this shower. So we had one bath during the four weeks we were there. And then again, we didn't have any cleaning, any water to clean ourselves or to drink at all. We got this coffee, and then we dispersed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came-- one day during my time there was an incident which happened. Well there were two incidents, which I want to tell about. The one incident was that we had amongst us a few youngsters. I thought actually up until now I thought they were 12-year-old. But I just found out that they were actually 14 by that time. But they were much smaller than-- they were youngsters for us still. And the kapo, who was a Hungarian kapo, wanted to give in front of me to one of these youngsters a much smaller piece of-- only a half a piece of bread. And I said, no, you are going to give her the whole one. And she said, I certainly won't. And I said, well, you are going to give her because I'm going to hit you if you are not going to. I'm going to take it away from you. Now, I am not a-- I didn't used to be an aggressive person, but I-- and, and actually, she gave her. And from that moment on, that kapo had such respect for me. She could have then sent me off under any pretext and got rid of me. And I must say I respected her, too, for that. I never had any more trouble with that kapo. She remained our kapo until, during the whole time in Auschwitz and during my whole time that I was in camp. And she and I never had any trouble or any dealings actually with her anymore. She was put on trial afterwards. But luckily I-- well I wasn't asked. But I didn't-- I certainly wouldn't have put her on trial on my account. Now she-- that was the one incident. And the other incident was that while we were outside and we weren't allowed into the barrack, and we were grilling under the sun. And very many people of course were completely burnt, and blistering, and in terrible agony. Somebody found a piece of glass. And with the piece of glass they made a circle, drew a circle. And then-- I never believed in anything spiritualism. And they, they started to do something, moving and reading odd things. And suddenly-- and I was just standing there. And suddenly one of them shouted, Hitler has been assassinated. Now we could not have got news. There was no way we could have got news at that point. Later on, there was possibility. But at that point there was absolutely no possibility. We were not taken out. We didn't have occasion to speak to anyone except to-- I mean, amongst us. To Germans you had no occasion to speak. There was Hitler has been assassinated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for some reason or other, and again I come back again, I was obsessed. I said, no, he was not. He was only-- he wasn't. Nothing happened to him or he was-- I don't remember now. Did I say he was hurt? But anyway, he didn't die. And I was nearly lynched because there was big scream, I mean, screaming Hitler has been assassinated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said no. And I insisted. I insisted. And it was actually the 20th July when he was attacked. And I don't know why I said it. I don't know why I-- I mean I didn't want to be lynched, but I mean, I was, I was adamant that nothing, that nothing happened to him. Now this was the second time. And I have a third time when this thing, this funny thing happened to me. And I am a very, very down to earth person and very-- who always faces up to everything. But but this, this happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's an unaccountable experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unaccountable experiences. And these were the two things. Now then about, I don't know, at midday, we had to again stand in rows of five. And then we were given our meal for the day. We were given-- first they brought a so-called soup. And it's the same thing happened as with the coffee again in a medium sized casserole. And there was some cabbage leaf floating in it or some potato floating in it, but I mean very little. The meat was usually taken out by the kapo and her family and, or the or the SS or the-- but anyway, it certainly didn't reach me. And again, I had hardly anything. And then we were given a piece of bread. And occasionally we were given a piece of cheese. And the cheese was extremely tasty but extremely salty, which made us, of course, even thirstier. Which-- well we were constantly thirsty, dirty, hungry, and had dysentery. There were rumors that they put something into our food that we had dysentery. There were rumors at the time also, at the beginning, that they put something into our food that we came, that we had menstruation, or that stopped the menstruation. Not that we had menstruation, but that it stopped the menstruation. I don't know. I don't know. They never have been substantiated. I never could find anything about it. Then we-- so this was our food. Now the, the soup we drank. The cheese we ate. But there was this piece of bread. And you didn't have anything more until next day, the piece of bread. So you would have loved to keep it but of course you had no pockets. You had nowhere where you could put it. You had nowhere where you could hide it. You knew if you dropped it or put it down somewhere somebody, one of your mates would straightaway take it and eat it without any conscience of guilt because they were also hungry. And when people are hungry, most people they just take. And-- so I-- so usually we ate it up because-- and then we had absolutely nothing more. I mean, it wasn't a large piece anyway. But one would have loved to keep it. And then in the evening we were again put in into the barracks. And that was a day gone. And that's how it went on with that one interruption until, I think, the 1st of August. We arrived in Auschwitz on the 7th of July, and I think it was the 1st of August that there was this big selection, when not only us-- Oh, there was one incident in before, which I want to tell. One day-- oh, that was also the first of August. On the 1st of August, in the early afternoon or in the morning, anyway after appell, after their having been counted, or between lunch-- I don't remember exactly the timing-- suddenly, I thought I am hallucinating because I heard as if I heard the from Mozart being played. I thought, well this is I am hallucinating. In this hell there can't possibly be Mozart's music. And I, I decided-- but I saw the women running in a certain direction. And I decided to go and have a look what is there. And as I went nearer and nearer, the music became louder, and there was an orchestra sitting there. And they played And then they played a couple of more things. And then they stopped. And I went up to the cellist, and I spoke to her a few words. And then they went off. And then we had, I don't know, lunch or whatever, I mean that one meal. And then there was a selection. In late afternoon there was a selection. Everybody from all the barracks in that, in Camp B, had to go to the main square to be counted. And we had to stand there. And we-- no, that was-- then this must have been on the 31st of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: July?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of July, of July. Because, yes, because the next morning we were, very early we were taken out of the barracks. And we had to go to the appellplatz for the main, main place, where, where about 15,000 of us were gathered. We all had to strip naked. And we stood there naked for the next 14 hours. It was cold. We were freezing. It got hot. Then it got cold again. And we were standing there. And then the camp commandant Hoess came, and the camp Dr. Mengele came. And they-- we had to pass in front of them. And they waved us right and left. And we, we had to show them our palm. And sometimes they said, open your mouth. And-- but otherwise, that's why we had to stand for 14 hours naked. And they, they didn't-- anybody who collapsed, of course, was taken away. And that was, that was finished. And then we-- I was taken into a row. We were led away. Yes, we were led out of the Birkenau B camp. And we were led into another part, which was Auschwitz A, which we found out it was Auschwitz A. And there we had to-- we could sit down there. And it got-- well, it was dark by that time, by the time we got there. And then suddenly we smelled some-- we were smelling some beautiful cabbage soup. And then someone came around in the dark. No, before-- sorry. Before that they took us again into this big hall and shaved us once more. And then gave us some clothing and-- fresh clothing. And no, before they gave us fresh clothing, they started tattooing us. And the tattooing, about 20 of us were tattooed and then there was an air raid.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your number that was tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a number that was tattooed. I don't have a number. And the reason-- I have a number. I have got it on every document which came out of Auschwitz. But I haven't got an actual number. I never have been tattooed because after 20 of us were about tattooed, there was an air raid. It was an air raid on the Buna Works, on the I.G. Farben, where they were doing-- preparing the Zyklon, producing the Zyklon B gas. And they-- so all the lights went out. And they stopped tattooing us. And they took us out, well chased us out, from this hall. And we sat down at a certain place they told us to sit down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came this terrific smell of this cabbage soup. And then suddenly someone, who we saw only the shadow, but we couldn't see their face at all. There was no moon. It was really very, very dark. They couldn't see the face of at all. And, and, and somebody was shouting, is there anyone from Szombathely here? And so I said, yes, I am here. And we spoke. And this was a girl who used to live in my town, but about 10 years about-- she was, I think, 12 years old-- we were the same age-- about 12 years old, when her father died and her mother and she moved to Budapest. And I never saw her since then. But I knew her as a child. We used to play together. So I remembered her. And now suddenly I heard her. And we were-- she asked is there anybody else from Szombathely? And she knew about some of my friends, who were in Auschwitz A camp. These are together with her. And she told me about them, what happened to them or what didn't happen to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they-- she gave me some soup. And then she went. She had to move on. And late-- and then when it got light-- I mean the soup was fantastic. It was the first decent soup with some taste.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other people with you get given soup too or only you because you knew her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were now a group of 1,000 women. As far as I know, we were only our group now already, which was-- which we were going to be 1,000 women. From now on we were 1,000 Hungarian Jewish women. And I don't know what happened to others, no idea. But in our group we were 1,000 Hungarian woman. We are sitting there on a strip somewhere. I don't know where. It was Auschwitz A camp. And it was night. And then when it got light, then they took us and they told-- and they got us into cattle trucks but not squeezed in, quite roomy. And there was straw on the ground. And they took us away. And we were transferred into a Buchenwald out camp, near a castle in a place called Hessisch Lichtenau. We arrived there. I don't know how long we travelled. I have no recollection of how long we travelled, but it wasn't too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that we got a piece of bread, a big piece of bread for the journey. We didn't know where they'd take us, no. They took us. We didn't know anything what's going to happen to us. We only knew that we were, we were being taking somewhere. And since they were all younger people there, we thought that most probably we were taken-- we were going to go to work somewhere. Because we looked all quite-- still, still we were still quite strong. I mean, we weren't that strong anymore, but we had already a few weeks of starvation. But I mean, we still were relatively strong. Anyway, they took us there. And they took us to Hessisch Lichtenau. And in Hessisch Lichtenau they took us into a camp, which when we saw it, we thought it was paradise. First of all, we had rooms. And in each room there where, I think, 32 beds or 36 beds-- not beds but bunks in two layers. And then we were-- and we were surrounded by grass and trees. It was a beautiful camp, really very, very friendly looking. We were-- we could-- we were taken into the bathroom, which had hot and cold water, had plugs. There were lots-- it was a row of white, clean, beautiful bowls, hot and cold water, a soap on each bowl, and a plug for each bowl. So we thought we were in paradise. And we were given somewhat some sort of meal. I mean, we thought it's marvelous. Now all our problems are over. And that's it. And now we are going to work. And we are going to be fed. And we are going to be looked after decently, and right. Anyway, some of my camp inmates-- I mean people like to have things, like to own things. And they don't like it if somebody else owns it. So to make it sure that they owned it, they took off the plugs so that they had the plugs. And they took away the soap that they had the soaps. And so for others there was no plug and there was no soap. And because that we were punished. And they closed down the bathroom. And when they occasionally opened it, then there was definitely no soap there, and definitely no plugs there, and definitely no hot water there. But this was how it was. I mean, some people just have to have things, have to own things. And so we could use the bathroom quite often, but not often enough. And so I felt rather dirty. And therefore, when later on when it's, when it came to winter and we had beautiful snow, I used to slip out of the barrack at night and strip and wash myself in snow because I felt that it was incredibly necessary, absolutely necessary to, to, to, to be clean, to keep oneself clean. And I mean I could have been shot at the spot that I came out of the barrack, which I was not supposed to come out of at night. But nobody did anything to me. And I felt a bit cleaner. We, in Hessisch Lichtenau, we also got some underwear. And people got shoes. Now I didn't get shoes because I always had very large feet. And unfortunately, the shoes they brought were not large enough. They were, I don't know, up to 6.5 or maximum 7 or something like that. And I had size 8 shoe, size 8 feet, or size 9 feet. In those days I think it was size 9. Today it's a bit less. And therefore. There were no shoes. There were occasionally there were clogs, which I was given. And the clogs had-- and occasionally, most of the time, with the clog, I also got a duster to put around my foot, to put into when I wear the clogs. But I had one roommate who always made it sure that my duster after the first night disappeared from under my head or wherever I hid it. She always managed to find it. And clogs were rather hard on, on bare feet. And were even harder-- and I mean, they broke very soon. So most of the time actually, summer and winter, I was barefoot, and in the snow as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I must stop for a moment. Can I stop for a moment? Do you need to just take a minute's break or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I must take a minute break. I'm a bit giddy now. [DRINKS] Oh, so actually-- oh, there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So most of the time I was barefoot during the next, I mean all my time in camp. But occasional I had these clogs, which of course in summer was bearable. In winter it was very, very hard to, to bear. Now we were-- I want to tell a little bit about life in camp. We-- as time went on-- we were there for 8 and 1/2 months-- and as time went on, food became less and less. And-- and we became weaker and weaker. We had a commandant. We had two people, two people in charge. Now, I always believed that the one was came from Buchenwald once a month. But I just now heard from other camp mates that he was always there. But I only remember him coming into camp for a couple of days from time to time and make terror because he was hitting people. He was terrible-- when-- and then-- when he was there-- I can't say exact things oddly enough. But I just remember that he terrorized us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Zorbach. And he just has been found. He just has been discovered. And we were just discussing in Budapest with some of my ex camp mates what should we do about it? Should we do something? He's 86 years old and he was in France under another name until now. And he's now back in Germany. We have his address. And we have-- but he was once already under-- I mean, he had already a trial. But that was Zorbach. Now the other one was-- the other commandant who was around, who was always there was Willi Schaffer. Now Willi Shaffer was a good looking man. He was-- I have very ambivalent feelings and the same as quite a few of my camp mates. As I say, I met some and we spoke about it just recently again. He was a man who did everything according to the rule. Now one day two of my camping mates escaped. And we were told that if you escape and you are being found, then you are shot, being shot. They were brought back. And we were called to the appell place. And we had to stand there and watch while they were digging their graves. And then they were shot into it. And we had to bury them. That was according to the rule. It was also according to rule that we had our bread ration. And every day we had our bread ration. Exactly the same bread ration, exactly at the same time, we had our bread ration. It became-- after a while it became later. As time went on it became a bit smaller and smaller, but we had, everybody had the equal piece of bread ration. Now one day Willi, as we called him, was called-- no before I tell that, I must tell one little story.
